transcript.text: Design a view count service for YouTube or Netflix: count views per video at massive scale, display near-real-time, resistant to inflation and double-counting; billions of views per day.

blocks.md:
# Design a View Count Service (YouTube / Netflix)

The core tension in one line: we have to swallow a **firehose of view-increments** — billions a day, with one viral video alone hammering a single key — while showing a count that looks *live* and is *hard to inflate*, and we have to do it cheaply. Counting sounds trivial; counting at this scale, accurately, in near-real-time, is a write-amplification and hot-key problem.

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and core entities, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the write firehose, the single hot video, and anti-inflation — checking in with you as I go. I'll keep cost and operations first-class throughout. Sound good?"

I'll target an **Amazon-style bar**: cost and operations are first-class, I work backwards from what the *viewer and creator* actually need, and I'll decide and move rather than study forever. Tell me if you'd rather I tilt Google (deeper on one consistency problem) or Meta (front-load the read hot-path).

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

### 🔒 Scope lock (decide these FIRST — vetoable assumptions, stop me if any is wrong)

These are the questions whose answers *fork the whole architecture*. I'm stating each as an assumption I'll build on, not an open question — veto any and I'll re-tilt.

1. **Approximate-but-fast beats exact-but-slow for display.** I'll assume the *displayed* count can be **eventually consistent** — a few seconds to a couple of minutes stale is fine (the number you see under a video). Exact-to-the-unit counting is only required for a *separate* monetization/billing pipeline, which I'll treat as a different, slower, reconciled system. *(If you tell me the displayed number must be exact-to-the-unit in real time, the design changes completely — that's a strongly-consistent global counter and a totally different cost curve.)*
2. **Writes dominate the hard part; reads dominate the volume.** I'll assume **~10 billion views/day** and a **read:write ratio around 20:1** (every view event is displayed many times across the watch page, search, feeds, and recommendations). So the *write firehose* is the correctness challenge and the *read fan-out* is the volume challenge.
3. **One video can be radically hotter than the rest.** I'll assume a **synchronized premiere / viral drop** can concentrate ~30% of peak traffic onto a *single video id*. That single hot key — not aggregate scale — is the binding bottleneck.
4. **A "view" needs a definition, and it must resist inflation.** I'll assume a view counts once per (user-or-device, video) inside a **dedup window** (say 24h), gated by a minimal watch signal, and that we actively defend against bots and refresh-spam.

> 🤝 **In/out of scope — add or cut before I commit:** IN — increment ingestion, dedup/anti-inflation, near-real-time display reads, the rolled-up total per video, and the hot-video case. OUT — the recommendation system, video storage/transcoding, the exact monetization ledger (separate reconciled pipeline), and per-second analytics dashboards (I'll mention how the raw log feeds them).

### Functional requirements (derived from the locks above — "above the line")

Since we assumed an approximate, write-heavy, hot-key-prone counter, the core flows are:

**FR1 — Record a view.** A client reports that a video was watched; the system counts it at most once per dedup window.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when someone watches a video, we bump that video's number — but if the same person refreshes ten times, it still only counts once.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the firehose — hundreds of thousands of increments a second, one video taking a huge slice, and we can't let a viral video's single counter become a single locked row.

**FR2 — Display a video's view count.** Anyone loading a video (or a feed/search result) sees its current count, near-real-time.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the number under the video shows up instantly and looks live, even if it's a few seconds behind the truth.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** millions of reads a second; the count must be servable from cache/edge without touching the write path, and it must only ever go *up*.

**FR3 — Resist inflation and double-counting.** Bots, refresh-spam, and retries must not pump the number.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if a bot farm or a buggy client hits us a million times, the count shouldn't jump a million.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** dedup at firehose scale needs a cheap per-(viewer, video) membership check, and abuse detection without blocking legitimate viral spikes.

**FR4 — Maintain a durable rolled-up total per video.** The cache/edge is backed by a system-of-record count that survives restarts and reconciles against the raw event log.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** even if every cache node reboots, we still know each video's real total.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the durable total must be assembled from many sharded sub-counters and the raw event stream, exactly-once enough that we don't drift over months.

**Below the line (out of scope):** recommendation ranking, video upload/transcoding/CDN delivery of the *media*, the exact ad-monetization ledger, watch-time analytics, comments/likes (a separate but structurally-similar counter), GDPR right-to-erasure mechanics (mentioned in ops), per-region legal data residency.

### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets, tagged to the assumption they lean on)

**NFR1 — Display reads: p99 ~50ms, 4 nines availability** *(assumes eventual-consistency display, lock #1).*
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the count almost always shows up in under a tenth of a second, and the read service is down less than ~1 hour/year total.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the count widget spins or shows nothing on every watch page — visible on the most-loaded surface in the product.

**NFR2 — Write ingestion: accept-and-ack in <10ms, never block on the count** *(assumes write firehose, lock #2).*
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** reporting a view returns instantly; we durably capture it and count it slightly later.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** under a premiere, the client hangs waiting for a contended counter and we drop views — undercounting the most important moment.

**NFR3 — Display freshness (staleness bound): count is at most ~60s behind truth at p99** *(assumes approximate display, lock #1).*
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the number you see is never more than about a minute old.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** creators and viewers see a "stuck" count during a viral moment and lose trust ("it's been at 1M for 10 minutes").

**NFR4 — Counting accuracy: ≥99.9% of legitimate, deduped views are eventually reflected; the count is monotonic (never decreases)** *(assumes approximate display + dedup, locks #1, #4).*
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we might be off by a hair in the moment, but we converge to the right number and it never visibly drops.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a count that ticks *down* is a public embarrassment and looks like fraud.

**NFR5 — Anti-inflation: dedup + abuse defense holds at firehose scale without false-blocking a real viral spike** *(assumes lock #4).*
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** spam doesn't inflate the number, but a genuinely viral video isn't mistaken for an attack.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** exactly-once at this volume is a fiction — I'm engineering *effectively-once* with idempotent dedup keys and a reconciled ground-truth log, and I'll quantify the residual drift. I'll revisit this in the ledger.

> 💬 "The whole game here is: take a brutal write firehose with one impossibly-hot key, make the *displayed* number cheap and near-live, and make it genuinely hard to inflate — while keeping the count from ever ticking down."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So the system records views, shows the count near-real-time, resists inflation, and keeps a durable total. The big calls I'm making up front: the *displayed* count can be a little stale — seconds to a minute — because exact-real-time counting at ten billion a day would be ruinously expensive and isn't what a viewer needs. Reads outnumber writes about twenty to one, so reads are my volume problem and writes are my correctness problem. And the thing that'll actually break me isn't aggregate scale — it's one premiere video swallowing a third of peak traffic on a single counter. Everything downstream is shaped by those three calls."

---

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

The residual of the scope lock — the calls most likely to be wrong, made explicit so you can redirect.

**Fork A — I assumed the displayed count can be eventually consistent (a few seconds to ~1 min stale).**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If it must be **exact-to-the-unit, real-time** → I drop the sharded approximate counters and the cache-first read path, and build a strongly-consistent global counter (consensus or a serialized increment log per video) — orders of magnitude more expensive, and the hot video becomes a hard single-writer ceiling I can't shard away.
  - If **even more relaxed** (counts can be minutes-to-hours stale, e.g. an internal dashboard) → I skip the real-time aggregation tier entirely and just batch-count the raw log.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm treating the number under the video as a near-real-time *estimate* that converges. If your product genuinely needs it exact to the unit in real time — say it's a contest with prizes — tell me and I'll build a very different, much pricier counter."

**Fork B — I assumed one video can take ~30% of peak (the hot-key fork).**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If traffic is **roughly uniform** across videos → I skip per-key counter sharding entirely; a plain sharded-by-video-id counter is fine and the design gets much simpler.
  - If it's **even more skewed** (a single global live event, 50%+) → I need adaptive/dynamic sub-counter splitting and a dedicated hot-key path, not a static shard count.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming one premiere can dominate. If your traffic is actually flat across videos, tell me and I'll drop the hot-key machinery — it's the most complex part and it's only earned by skew."

**Fork C — I assumed a "view" is deduped per (viewer, video) in a window.**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If a view is **every play, no dedup** (e.g. a raw impression counter) → I drop the dedup store entirely; ingestion gets simpler and cheaper but the number is trivially inflatable.
  - If dedup is **stricter** (per logged-in user, lifetime, + watch-time threshold) → the dedup store grows unbounded and I need a different membership structure (probabilistic + cold storage).
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm deduping per device-or-user inside a 24-hour window. If your definition of a view is looser or stricter, that mostly changes the dedup store, not the rest of the architecture."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three places my design is sensitive. Are you happy with eventual-consistency display and a hot-key assumption, or do you want to push on exact real-time counting before I size it?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

Computed by running `capacity.py`; only rounded, say-out-loud figures surfaced. Glosses inline.

**QPS** — *queries per second, how many requests hit us each second.* **Burst factor** — *peak ÷ average; I use 4x for prime-time plus viral spikes.* **Cache hit ratio** — *fraction of count-reads served from memory/edge without touching the database.*

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Avg write QPS | 10B views/day ÷ 86,400s = 115,741/s | ~116K/s | "about a hundred thousand a second, steady" | Ingestion must be async + batched; no synchronous DB write per view |
| **Peak write QPS** | 116K/s × 4 burst = 462,963/s | **~463K/s** | "near half a million increments a second at peak" | Sharded counters + a durable log buffer; cannot land on one row |
| **Hot-video QPS** | 463K/s × 30% premiere share = 138,889/s | **~139K/s** | "one video alone doing ~140 thousand a second" | **Binding bottleneck — exceeds single-key ceiling → shard ONE key** |
| Single Redis key ceiling | ~100K ops/s on one key/one shard (gloss below) | ~100K/s | "one counter tops out near a hundred thousand" | The flip line: any video over this forces sub-counter fan-out |
| Avg read QPS | 116K/s × 20 read:write = 2,314,815/s | ~2.3M/s | "a couple million count-reads a second" | Reads MUST be cache/edge-served, not DB-served |
| Peak read QPS | 2.3M/s × 4 burst = 9,259,259/s | ~9.3M/s | "about nine million reads a second at peak" | CDN + in-memory read tier; DB never on the hot read path |
| Miss read QPS (DB sees) | 9.3M/s × (1 − 0.99 hit) = 92,593/s | ~93K/s | "only ~90 thousand reads a second reach the store" | Read replica/store sized from MISSES, not from 9.3M |
| Cold-cache worst case | 0% hit → full 9.3M/s to origin | ~9.3M/s | "if cache dies cold, nine million hit the floor" | Need request-coalescing + stale-serve to survive a cold flush |
| Dedup store size | 10B keys/day × 64 B, 24h TTL | ~640 GB | "well under a terabyte of dedup keys" | Fits an in-memory/SSD KV cluster; non-issue |
| **Raw event log** | 10B/day × 200 B × 3 replicas × 1.5 headroom = 9 TB/day → 270 TB/mo → 0.8 PB @ 90d | **~270 TB/mo** | "about a quarter petabyte a month" | Largest table → partition-drop retention, tiered to object store |
| Rolled-up count table | 2B videos × 100 B × 3 = 600 GB | ~600 GB | "the totals themselves are tiny" | The count table is a non-issue; the *log* is the cost |
| Monthly cost | hot storage + log stream + cache fleet ≈ $114K | ~$0.1M/mo | "low six figures a month" | Cache+aggregator fleet dominates, not storage |
| $ per 1M views | $114K/mo ÷ 300B views/mo × 1M | ~$0.38 | "well under a dollar per million views" | Unit economics — cheap per view, watch the fleet |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **hot video at ~139K/s vs the ~100K/s single-key ceiling.** That single comparison is the whole design. **Flip threshold:** if the hottest single video stays *under* ~100K/s, I drop per-key sub-counter sharding and a plain counter-per-video is enough. Above it, I *must* fan one logical counter across multiple physical sub-counters and sum on read.

> 🧠 *Single-key ceiling gloss:* a Redis (in-memory key-value store) key lives on exactly one shard/CPU, so one counter is limited by one core's atomic-increment throughput — empirically low-hundreds-of-thousands/s before tail latency blows up. You can't replica-scale a single hot *write* key; you have to split it.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number I actually care about is one video doing about a hundred-and-forty thousand increments a second during a premiere, against a single counter that tops out near a hundred thousand. That one comparison forces the whole design — I have to split a single hot counter across several physical counters and add them up on read. On the read side it's nine million a second at peak, but with a 99% cache hit ratio only about ninety thousand of those actually reach the store, so I'll spend my budget on a cache and aggregation fleet, not on a bigger database. Storage is a rounding error next to that fleet."

---

## 4. Core Entities

A first-draft scaffold — full fields in §7.

**ViewEvent** — one reported watch: who/what device, which video, when, source surface, and a dedup key. It OWNS the raw, append-only truth.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a turnstile click — every single entry is logged, even if we later decide it didn't "count."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "isn't logging every event wasteful?" → it's our reconciliation ground-truth and the input to analytics; it's append-only and aggressively tiered to cheap storage.

**VideoCounter** — the *logical* current total for a video. It OWNS the displayed number and is physically split into sub-counters for hot videos.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a stadium with several entrances each with its own clicker; the "attendance" is the sum of all clickers.
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "how do you keep it from ticking down?" → reads take the max of (cached, freshly-summed) and the durable total; sub-counters only ever increment.

**DedupKey** — a short-lived membership marker for (viewer-or-device, video) in the window. It OWNS "have we already counted this watch?"
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a hand-stamp at a club — once you're stamped, re-entry in the window doesn't re-charge you.
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "what about memory blowup?" → it's TTL'd (24h) and probabilistic-friendly; ~640 GB total, trivially cluster-able.

**VideoCountSnapshot** — the durable, reconciled system-of-record total per video, periodically flushed from sub-counters and corrected against the event log. It OWNS survivability.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** the official audited attendance posted after the gates close, reconciling all the clickers.
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "what if a cache node dies mid-flush?" → the snapshot is the floor; we re-derive the lost delta from the raw log.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four entities. The ViewEvent is the raw append-only log — every click, our ground truth. The VideoCounter is the live number, and the surprising part is it's not one counter — for a hot video it's several sub-counters we sum on read, so no single key melts. The DedupKey is a short-lived stamp that stops double-counting. And the Snapshot is the durable audited total we reconcile against the log. I'll put real fields and shard keys on these in the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

All endpoints under `/v1`. **Versioning:** additive fields don't bump the version; a breaking change ships as `/v2` with overlap. **Pagination:** any list endpoint (admin/analytics) uses an opaque cursor token, never offset/limit — offset gets slower and skips or dupes rows as data shifts underneath it. **Security:** the caller's identity comes from the session/JWT (*a signed token proving who you are*), never a client-supplied user id; object-level authz is checked per request.

### FR1 — Record a view → `POST /v1/videos/{videoId}/views`
POST because it creates new state (a counted event). The client reports *that* a view happened; it does NOT send the count or a user id we'd trust.
- `dedupKey: string` — client-generated stable id per watch session (device id + video + window bucket), so retries collapse. *Why:* makes the write idempotent.
- `watchSignal: object` — minimal proof of a real play (e.g. ms-watched, player heartbeat). *Why:* anti-inflation gate; absent → not counted.
- `source: string` — surface (watch, feed, search). *Why:* analytics + abuse signal.
- **Absent on purpose:** no `userId` (taken from JWT or device attestation), no `count`, no `timestamp` we trust (server stamps ingest time).

```
// Request
POST /v1/videos/vid_9a3f/views
Idempotency-Key: dk_8f2c1e
{ "dedupKey": "dk_8f2c1e", "watchSignal": { "msWatched": 4200 }, "source": "watch" }

// Response 202
{ "accepted": true, "videoId": "vid_9a3f", "counted": "deferred" }
```
- **Key decision:** returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 with a count. We durably buffer the event and count asynchronously — this keeps ingest under 10ms and never blocks on a contended hot counter. The `Idempotency-Key`/`dedupKey` makes retries safe: a duplicate returns the same 202 without recounting.

### FR2 — Display the count → `GET /v1/videos/{videoId}/count`
GET because it only reads. No body.
- Returns the near-real-time count plus a freshness hint.
```
// Request
GET /v1/videos/vid_9a3f/count

// Response 200
{ "videoId": "vid_9a3f", "count": 14820391, "asOf": "2026-06-21T18:04:02Z", "approx": true }
```
- **Key decision:** `approx: true` and `asOf` make the staleness *explicit* to clients (NFR3). The value is the **max** of cached and durable-floor, so it's monotonic — it never ticks down.

### FR2b — Batch counts for a feed → `GET /v1/videos/counts?ids=...`
GET, multi-key; the read path for feeds/search where one page shows many counts.
```
// Request
GET /v1/videos/counts?ids=vid_9a3f,vid_77b2,vid_0c41

// Response 200
{ "counts": { "vid_9a3f": 14820391, "vid_77b2": 22045, "vid_0c41": 901233 }, "asOf": "2026-06-21T18:04:02Z" }
```
- **Key decision:** one multi-get amortizes the per-page fan-out; coalesced behind the same cache. Object-level authz: only counts for videos the caller can *see* are returned (private/unlisted filtered out).

### FR3/FR4 — Admin: reconciled true total → `GET /v1/admin/videos/{videoId}/count?exact=true`
GET, privileged. Returns the reconciled snapshot (slower, from the log). Object-level authz: caller must hold the `analytics:read` scope for that video's channel — "can THIS caller read THIS channel's exact numbers?", not just "is the caller logged in?"

> 💬 "The whole API is shaped by one idea: writes return instantly with a 202 and an idempotency key, reads return an explicitly-approximate, monotonic number with an `asOf` stamp."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Recording a view is a POST that returns 202 — accepted, counted later — carrying an idempotency key so retries don't double-count. Reading is a GET that returns the number plus an `asOf` timestamp and an `approx` flag, so clients know it's near-live, not exact. There's a batch read for feeds. And there's a privileged admin endpoint for the reconciled exact total. I never trust a client-supplied user id, count, or timestamp — identity's from the JWT, the count's ours, and the server stamps the time."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 — Record a view (FR1)

The slice that swallows the firehose. The goal: accept ~463K writes/s peak, dedup them, and feed counters — without ever blocking the client or landing a hot video on one row.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest gateway | Thin stateless service that validates the event, checks dedup, and hands off | Need <10ms ack and abuse filtering at the edge before anything durable | Not writing straight to DB (a per-view DB insert at 463K/s melts any primary) | One more hop; mitigated by it being stateless and cheap |
| Durable event log (Kafka) | A append-only log that buffers a firehose so nothing's lost if a consumer lags | Decouples ingest speed from counting speed; replayable for reconciliation | Not an in-memory queue (loses events on crash); not direct-to-DB (no buffering) | Counting is now async → display is eventually consistent (we chose that) |
| Dedup store (Redis) | In-memory KV that remembers (viewer, video) for the TTL window | Need a sub-ms "seen this already?" check at firehose rate | Not a SQL unique index (a contended write per event); not Bloom-only (false-positive undercounts) | Memory cost (~640 GB) and TTL eviction tuning |
| Aggregator / counter workers | Consumers that read the log and increment sharded counters | Batch many increments into few counter ops; own the hot-key fan-out | Not incrementing per-event (no batching → hot key melts) | Adds counting latency (seconds), bounded by NFR3 |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "A view comes into a thin stateless ingest gateway — its whole job is to validate the watch signal, check the dedup stamp, and ack in under ten milliseconds. It does *not* touch the database; instead it appends to a durable log, Kafka, which is just an append-only buffer that holds events safely even if the thing counting them falls behind. The dedup check lives in Redis, an in-memory store, because I need a 'have I seen this watch?' answer in well under a millisecond and a SQL unique index would mean a contended write on every single event. Downstream, aggregator workers drain the log and batch increments into the counters — batching is what turns a 463-thousand-a-second firehose into a manageable number of counter operations."

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable append-only log that buffers a stream of events so a slow consumer never loses data. Here it absorbs the view firehose and lets counting run asynchronously.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store with microsecond reads. Here it holds the short-lived dedup stamps so we can reject double-counts at firehose speed.
> **Dedup (deduplication)** — collapsing repeated reports of the same watch into one. Here it's the anti-inflation gate keyed on (viewer-or-device, video).
> **Aggregator worker** — a consumer process that reads the log and folds many increments into a few counter writes. Here it's what makes the hot-key batching possible.

**Write-path flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1 POST view"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    subgraph Ingest["Ingest path"]
        GW -->|"2 check dedup"| Dedup[(Redis dedup)]
        GW -->|"3 append event"| Log[[Kafka view log]]
    end
    subgraph Count["Async counting"]
        Log -->|"4 consume batch"| Agg[Aggregator worker]
        Agg -->|"5 incr sub-counter"| Ctr[(Redis sharded counters)]
        Agg -->|"6 flush snapshot"| Snap[(Count snapshot store)]
    end
    Ctr -.->|"7 serves reads"| ReadTier[Read tier]
```
↳ reuses existing: none — this is the foundational slice.

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client POSTs the view.** The gateway owns admission; it's stateless so we scale it horizontally. *What could go wrong:* a flood of bot traffic → rate-limit at the edge and require a watch signal (§8.3).
2. **Dedup check in Redis.** If the (viewer, video) stamp exists in the window, we ack 202 and drop the event silently — it was already counted. *State change:* none if duplicate; otherwise the stamp is set. *Risk:* Redis dedup down → fail-open (count, risk small over-count) is safer than fail-closed (lose real views); see §9B.
3. **Append to Kafka.** The event is now durable; the client gets its 202. *State change:* event committed to the log. *Risk:* Kafka unavailable → gateway buffers to local disk and retries; back-pressure if log is full (§9).
4. **Aggregator consumes a batch.** Workers pull, say, 50ms windows of events. *Why:* batching collapses thousands of increments per video into one counter op. *Risk:* worker lag → freshness slips toward the 60s bound; alert on consumer lag.
5. **Increment a sub-counter.** For a hot video, the worker picks one of N physical sub-counters (round-robin / by partition) so no single key sees the full ~139K/s. *State change:* sub-counter += batch delta.
6. **Flush to the snapshot store.** Periodically the summed sub-counters are written to a durable snapshot (system-of-record). *State change:* durable total advances. *Risk:* crash mid-flush → re-derive from log offset.
7. **Counters serve the read tier** (covered in 6.2).

> 💬 "The trick on the write side: we never count synchronously. We durably *capture* in a log and count in batches downstream — that's what lets one ack be 10ms while one video does 140K a second."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Writes go to a thin gateway that dedups and appends to Kafka, then acks — done in milliseconds, nothing blocks. Asynchronously, aggregator workers drain the log and fold batches of increments into sharded counters, so even a viral video's flood lands across several physical counters instead of melting one. The deliberate trade-off is that the displayed number lags a few seconds — which we already decided is fine."

### 6.2 — Display the count (FR2)

The volume slice: ~9.3M reads/s peak, must be cache/edge-served, monotonic, and explicitly approximate.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN / edge cache | Geographically-distributed caches near users | Serve the count from the edge with a short TTL so most reads never reach us | Not origin-only (9.3M/s would crush any fleet) | Edge staleness adds to the freshness budget (bounded TTL) |
| Read tier (count API) | Stateless service summing sub-counters + snapshot floor | Owns the monotonic max-merge and the approx/asOf contract | Not reading the DB directly (misses would still be ~93K/s of joins) | Slight compute to merge sub-counters per read |
| Sharded counters (Redis) | Same counters the aggregators write | In-memory sum of sub-counters is sub-ms | Not recomputing from the log on read (way too slow) | Must reconcile against snapshot to avoid drift |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Reads are answered as close to the user as possible — a CDN edge cache with a short time-to-live serves the overwhelming majority, so our own fleet only sees the misses, about ninety thousand a second. On a miss, a stateless read service sums the video's sub-counters from Redis and takes the max against the durable snapshot floor, so the number is always monotonic and never ticks down. We deliberately don't read the database on the hot path — the snapshot is a floor, not the live source."

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a fleet of caches spread worldwide that serve responses near the user. Here it absorbs the bulk of the ~9.3M reads/s so origin sees only misses.
> **TTL (time-to-live)** — how long a cached value stays valid before refetch. Here a short count TTL trades a little staleness for huge read offload.
> **Max-merge** — taking the larger of two candidate values. Here it guarantees the displayed count only ever increases.

**Read-path flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1 GET count"| CDN[CDN edge cache]
    CDN -->|"2 miss to origin"| Read[Read tier]
    subgraph Origin["Origin read path"]
        Read -->|"3 sum sub-counters"| Ctr[(Redis sharded counters)]
        Read -->|"4 read floor"| Snap[(Count snapshot store)]
    end
    Read -->|"5 max-merge result"| CDN
    CDN -->|"6 return count"| Client
```
↳ reuses existing: Redis sharded counters and snapshot store (from §6.1).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client GETs the count**, usually as part of a page/feed. *Risk:* burst of cold reads after a TTL flush → request coalescing (single-flight) so only one miss per key hits origin (§8.2).
2. **CDN miss → origin.** ~99% are hits and stop here. *State change:* none; pure read.
3. **Sum sub-counters in Redis.** The read tier adds the N physical sub-counters for that video. *Risk:* a sub-counter node down → use last-known + snapshot floor; count may briefly under-report, never over.
4. **Read the snapshot floor.** The durable total guarantees we never serve below the last reconciled value.
5. **Max-merge** the live sum and the floor → the served value. *State change:* none.
6. **Return + cache** at the edge with the short TTL and `asOf`.

> 💬 "Reads are monotonic by construction — we always serve the max of what's live and what's durably known, so the number can only climb."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The count is served from the edge with a short TTL, so most of nine-million-a-second reads never touch us. On a miss, we sum the video's sub-counters and take the max against the durable floor — that max is what makes the number monotonic. We tag it with an `asOf` time and an approx flag so clients know it's near-live, not exact, and we cache it back at the edge."

### 6.3 — Resist inflation / double-counting (FR3)

The integrity slice — woven onto the write path. New: a watch-signal gate, the dedup store (already drawn), and an async abuse-scoring lane that can claw back inflated deltas.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Watch-signal gate | A rule at ingest requiring minimal real-play proof | Cheapest filter: kills trivial bot/refresh spam before the log | Not counting every POST (trivially inflatable) | Tunable threshold can drop borderline real views |
| Async abuse scorer | A stream job scoring patterns (rate, device, geo) | Catch coordinated inflation the cheap gate misses, without blocking viral spikes | Not synchronous ML at ingest (adds latency, false-blocks virality) | Detection lags; corrections are after-the-fact |
| Correction stream | Negative deltas reconciled into snapshots only | Remove fraud from the system-of-record without making the *display* tick down | Not subtracting from live display (violates monotonicity) | True total and display diverge briefly until reconciliation |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Inflation defense is layered. First, a cheap watch-signal gate at ingest — no real play, no count — which kills the bulk of refresh-spam for free. Second, an asynchronous abuse scorer reads the event stream and flags coordinated patterns; I keep this off the hot path on purpose, because a synchronous fraud check would add latency and risk mistaking a genuinely viral video for an attack. When fraud is confirmed, corrections go into the durable snapshot, never the live display — so the public number never visibly drops."

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Watch signal** — minimal evidence a real play happened (ms watched, a heartbeat). Here it's the first, cheapest anti-inflation gate.
> **Idempotency key** — a stable id that makes a repeated request a no-op. Here the dedupKey collapses client retries so they count once.
> **Crypto-shredding** — deleting an encryption key to render data unreadable. Mentioned for PII erasure on the append-only log (§9).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1 POST with signal"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    GW -->|"2 gate + dedup"| Dedup[(Redis dedup)]
    GW -->|"3 append clean event"| Log[[Kafka view log]]
    Log -->|"4 tee to scorer"| Scorer[Async abuse scorer]
    Scorer -->|"5 flag fraud"| Corr[[Correction stream]]
    Corr -->|"6 reconcile down"| Snap[(Count snapshot store)]
```
↳ reuses existing: Ingest gateway, Redis dedup, Kafka log, snapshot store (from §6.1).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **POST carries a watch signal.** *State change:* none yet.
2. **Gate + dedup.** No signal or already-stamped → silently dropped. *Risk:* over-strict gate undercounts; tune against measured legit-view distributions.
3. **Append clean event** to the log.
4. **Tee to the abuse scorer** (a separate consumer; the count path doesn't wait on it).
5. **Flag fraud** → emit negative deltas to a correction stream.
6. **Reconcile down** into the snapshot only — display never decreases.

> 💬 "Fraud is removed from the *truth*, never from the *display* — that's how we stay both honest and monotonic."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Anti-inflation is three layers: a cheap watch-signal gate and dedup at ingest, then an async scorer that catches coordinated abuse off the hot path so we never false-block a viral moment, and finally corrections that adjust the durable total but never the live number — so the count stays both honest and monotonic."

### 6.4 — Durable rolled-up total (FR4)

The survivability slice. New: periodic snapshot flush with log offsets, and a batch reconciler that replays the log to correct drift.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Snapshot store (Cassandra) | Wide-column store keyed by videoId holding the durable total + log offset | Need high write throughput for snapshot flushes, simple key lookups, multi-region | Not a single SQL primary (snapshot flush write rate + global scale) | Eventual consistency across regions; we accept it |
| Batch reconciler | A periodic job that re-aggregates the raw log per video | Ground-truth correction of any drift between fast counters and the log | Not trusting counters forever (small drifts accumulate over months) | Reconciliation is hours-stale; that's fine for the floor |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The durable total lives in Cassandra, a wide-column store that takes heavy writes and does fast key lookups across regions — that fits snapshot flushes and per-video reads. Each snapshot is written with the Kafka log offset it covers, so after any crash we know exactly where to resume and re-derive lost deltas. A slower batch reconciler periodically replays the raw log to recompute true totals and correct any accumulated drift — the counters are fast but the log is truth."

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Cassandra** — a distributed wide-column database built for high write throughput and key-based reads across many nodes/regions. Here it's the durable snapshot system-of-record.
> **Log offset** — a position marker in Kafka. Here it makes snapshot flushes resumable and exactly-once-ish after a crash.
> **Reconciliation** — recomputing the authoritative number from raw data. Here a batch job replays the log to correct counter drift.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Log[[Kafka view log]]
    Ctr[(Redis sharded counters)]
    subgraph Flush["Snapshot flush"]
        Ctr -->|"1 periodic sum"| Flusher[Snapshot flusher]
        Flusher -->|"2 write total + offset"| Snap[(Cassandra snapshot)]
    end
    subgraph Reconcile["Batch reconcile"]
        Log -->|"3 replay per video"| Recon[Batch reconciler]
        Recon -->|"4 correct drift"| Snap
    end
    Snap -.->|"5 floor for reads"| Read[Read tier]
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka log, Redis counters, read tier (from §6.1/6.2).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Periodic sum** of sub-counters by a flusher.
2. **Write total + log offset** to Cassandra. *State change:* durable floor advances.
3. **Batch reconciler replays the log** per video (e.g. hourly/daily).
4. **Correct drift** in the snapshot if counters and log disagree. *Risk:* reconciler bug → versioned snapshots + audit; never below previous floor.
5. **Snapshot is the read floor** (ties back to 6.2 step 4).

> 💬 "Counters are fast and lossy; the log is slow and true — the snapshot is where we marry them."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The durable total lives in Cassandra, flushed periodically from the counters with the log offset stamped on it so crashes are resumable. A slower batch job replays the raw log to recompute truth and correct any drift the fast counters accumulated. That snapshot is the floor the read path never serves below."

### Final (high-level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1 GET count"| CDN[CDN edge cache]
    Client -.->|"2 POST view"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    subgraph ReadPath["Read path"]
        CDN -->|"3 miss"| Read[Read tier]
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Read path: the count comes from the edge; on a miss we sum the sub-counters and max-merge against the Cassandra floor, so it's fast and monotonic. Write path: a view is deduped, appended to Kafka, and counted in batches across sharded counters — never blocking the client. Async correctness: the log feeds an abuse scorer and a batch reconciler that adjust the durable snapshot, plus periodic counter flushes with log offsets — so we converge to truth and survive any node dying."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — Entity table**

| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ViewEvent | eventId (PK), videoId, viewerId/deviceId, ts, source, dedupKey, watchMs | Kafka log + object store (Parquet) | videoId (log partition) | At-least-once (dedup downstream) |
| VideoCounter (sub-counters) | videoId + shardIdx (PK), delta, updatedAt | Redis cluster | videoId (hashed; hot videos fan to N shardIdx) | Eventual, monotonic |
| DedupKey | dedupKey (PK = hash(viewerId,videoId,window)) | Redis cluster (TTL 24h) | dedupKey hash | Best-effort (fail-open) |
| VideoCountSnapshot | videoId (PK), total, logOffset, version, updatedAt | Cassandra | videoId | Eventual, monotonic floor |

**PART B — Storage decision cards**

**🗄️ Kafka (+ object store) — used for: ViewEvent / raw log**
1. **What it is:** a durable, partitioned, replayable append-only log; older segments are tiered to cheap object storage (S3) as Parquet for analytics.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** we need to *accept ~463K writes/s without losing any*, decouple counting from ingest, and *replay* the exact event history for reconciliation. A log is the only thing that does all three.
3. **Considered & rejected:** **Direct DB inserts** — 463K/s of inserts melts any primary and gives no replay; **an in-memory queue** — loses everything on crash, fatal for our reconciliation floor; **Kinesis** — fine, but Kafka's replay/offset semantics and tiered storage fit better and avoid lock-in.
4. **Trade-off:** counting is now async → the displayed count is eventually consistent (we chose that in §1).
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The raw views go into Kafka, basically a durable tape of every event. It lets me ack writes in milliseconds, count them in batches later, and replay the whole tape if I ever need to recompute the truth. The cost is the display lags a few seconds — which is fine."

**🗄️ Redis cluster — used for: sub-counters + dedup keys**
1. **What it is:** an in-memory key-value store with atomic increments and microsecond ops.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** the live count needs *atomic increments at sub-ms latency*, and dedup needs a *sub-ms membership check*; only memory hits those rates. Sub-counter fan-out (multiple keys per hot video) is a natural fit.
3. **Considered & rejected:** **A SQL counter row** — one row per video is a single hot lock at 139K/s, exactly the bottleneck we're avoiding; **DynamoDB atomic counters** — capped per-partition throughput and the same single-hot-key ceiling; **Memcached** — no atomic increment + persistence story.
4. **Trade-off:** in-memory is volatile → we must reconcile against Kafka + the snapshot floor so a node loss doesn't lose the total.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The live counters and the dedup stamps live in Redis because I need atomic increments and membership checks in well under a millisecond. The catch is it's in-memory, so I treat it as fast-but-lossy and back it with the durable snapshot and the log."

**🗄️ Cassandra — used for: VideoCountSnapshot (system-of-record total)**
1. **What it is:** a distributed wide-column database tuned for heavy writes and key lookups across regions.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** snapshot flushes are a steady stream of per-video writes and reads are simple `videoId → total` lookups, globally — Cassandra's write throughput and multi-region replication fit exactly.
3. **Considered & rejected:** **PostgreSQL** — great for transactions but a single primary can't take the global snapshot write rate and isn't natively multi-region; **DynamoDB** — viable, but Cassandra avoids vendor lock-in and we already operate it; **just trusting Redis** — volatile, no durable floor.
4. **Trade-off:** eventual consistency across regions and no rich queries — we don't need joins here.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The durable total sits in Cassandra — it eats per-video writes all day and does fast key lookups across regions. I considered Postgres but a single primary can't take the global write rate, and this is just a key-to-number map, so I don't need transactions."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why that level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Record view (increment) | Kafka → Redis | At-least-once → eventual | Never blocks ingest; dedup + reconciliation make it effectively-once |
| Display count (read) | CDN → Redis + Cassandra | Eventual, monotonic | Viewer needs near-live, not exact; max-merge guarantees no decrease |
| Read-your-own-writes | Redis sub-counter | Bounded staleness (~seconds) | After your view, your refresh may not show +1 for a few seconds — acceptable for a public count; see note |
| Durable total / reconcile | Cassandra | Eventual, audited floor | Truth converges via log replay; floor never regresses |
| Exact admin total | Cassandra (reconciled) + log | Strong-ish (point-in-time) | Monetization-adjacent; computed from the log, slower |

> **Read-your-own-writes note:** because counting is async, a user who just contributed a view may not see the +1 immediately. For a *public aggregate* count this is fine and expected. If a product surface needs it (e.g. "your view counted"), we can optimistically +1 on the *client* for that user's own action without touching the global counter.

**PART D — Data lifecycle (largest table: the raw event log)**
At **~270 TB/month** stored, a **90-day** hot retention caps us at **~0.8 PB**; without retention we grow unbounded. Reclamation is by **time-partitioned segments with a partition/segment DROP (O(1), instant)** — never row-by-row DELETE, which churns indexes and never cleanly reclaims space. Cold segments tier to **object storage (S3/Glacier)** as Parquet for analytics and any audit/reconciliation beyond 90 days, at a fraction of the hot cost. PII erasure on this append-only log uses **crypto-shredding** — drop the per-user key so the rows become unreadable rather than physically deleting from an immutable log.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Three stores, each for a job. Kafka is the durable tape of raw events — it buffers the firehose and lets me replay truth. Redis holds the live counters and dedup stamps because only memory does atomic increments in sub-milliseconds, but I treat it as fast-and-lossy. Cassandra holds the durable per-video total as the floor reads never drop below. I considered a single Postgres counter row early on and rejected it immediately — that one row is exactly the hot-key bottleneck the whole design exists to avoid. The raw log is the cost driver, so it's time-partitioned with instant segment drops at ninety days and tiered to cheap object storage after."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one number — one video at ~139K/s vs a ~100K/s single-key ceiling — and reason forward from "how do I not melt one counter."

### How do we count one viral video doing ~139K writes/s? (the binding bottleneck)

#### Bad: single counter row / single Redis key
**🗣️ Plain words:** keep one number per video and add one to it every time.
**Approach:** one `videoId → count` row (SQL) or one Redis key, `INCR` per view.
**Why people try it:** it's the obvious mental model — one video, one counter.
**⚠️ What breaks:** one key lives on one shard/core. At **~139K/s** against a **~100K/s** single-key ceiling, increments queue, tail latency explodes, and aggregator lag blows past the 60s freshness bound. A SQL row is worse — row-lock contention serializes 139K writers behind one lock.
**🔁 Forces upgrade:** any single video exceeding ~100K/s — exactly our premiere case.

#### Good: shard one counter into N sub-counters, sum on read
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad funneled the whole hot video through one physical key; here I *split that one logical counter into several physical sub-counters* so no single key sees the full rate.
**🗣️ Plain words:** give the hot video several clickers instead of one, and add them up when someone asks.
**Approach:** for videoId, write to `videoId:shard{0..N-1}`, picking the shard by aggregator partition or round-robin. Read sums the N keys. With N=3, ~139K/s ÷ 3 ≈ ~46K/s per key — comfortably under the ceiling.
**⚠️ What breaks:** a *static* N is wrong for most videos (a cold video doesn't need 100 sub-counters — that's 100 reads to display a count of 12) and too small for a once-a-year mega-event. Read cost grows with N.
**🔁 Forces upgrade:** skew that varies by 5+ orders of magnitude between cold and hot videos — you can't pick one N.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Agg[Aggregator workers]
    Agg -->|"incr shard 0"| S0[(counter shard 0)]
    Agg -->|"incr shard 1"| S1[(counter shard 1)]
    Agg -->|"incr shard 2"| S2[(counter shard 2)]
    Read[Read tier]
    S0 -->|"sum"| Read
    S1 -->|"sum"| Read
    S2 -->|"sum"| Read
```

#### Great: adaptive sub-counter fan-out with batched increments
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** a static N can't fit both a 12-view video and a 140K/s premiere; here N *adapts* to observed heat, and aggregators *batch* so even the hot shard sees few ops.
**🗣️ Plain words:** cold videos use one clicker, and a video automatically grows more clickers as it heats up — and we add up many views at once before touching any clicker.
**Approach:** (1) Aggregators batch increments over ~50ms windows, so 139K/s becomes a few thousand *counter ops*/s, not 139K. (2) A heat tracker promotes a video to N sub-counters when its rate crosses thresholds (e.g. 1 → 8 → 64), demoting when it cools. Read cost stays bounded because only hot videos have many shards, and their N is read from the snapshot metadata.
**Why this is the ceiling-raiser:** batching alone cuts the per-key rate ~10-50x; fan-out spreads the residual. New ceiling: limited only by total Redis cluster throughput, not one key.

**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** raw ~139K/s ÷ 50ms batch (≈ batches of ~7K views) ≈ a few thousand counter ops/s, then ÷ N=8 sub-counters ≈ a few hundred ops/s per key — ~200x under the single-key ceiling. Read cost for a hot video: 8 key-sums per miss, and misses are ~93K/s globally, not per video.

**✅ Failure matrix**
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Sub-counter node dies | Read serves sum of survivors + snapshot floor → brief under-report, never over; lost delta re-derived from log |
| Aggregator lag spikes | Freshness slips toward 60s bound → alert on consumer lag, scale workers |
| Promotion races (two workers split) | Idempotent metadata write; reads sum whatever shards exist (extra empty shards read 0) |
| Demotion while hot | Floor at current N; only demote after sustained cool-down to avoid thrash |
| Whole Redis cluster cold-flushes | Read tier serves snapshot floor; counters rewarm from log replay (RPO via log) |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Heat[Heat tracker]
    Heat -->|"promote hot video"| Meta[(Shard-count metadata)]
    Agg[Aggregator workers]
    Agg -->|"read N"| Meta
    Agg -->|"batched incr across N"| Ctr[(Redis sub-counters)]
    Read[Read tier]
    Read -->|"read N then sum"| Meta
    Read -->|"sum N shards"| Ctr
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The hot video can't sit on one counter — one key tops out near a hundred thousand a second and a premiere does forty percent more than that. So I do two things: aggregators batch increments over fifty-millisecond windows, which collapses a hundred-and-forty-thousand-a-second firehose into a few thousand counter operations, and then I fan one logical counter across several physical sub-counters and sum them on read. The clever part is making the number of sub-counters *adaptive* — a cold video uses one, a viral one automatically grows to dozens as it heats up — so I never pay to read sixty-four keys for a video that has twelve views."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just add Redis replicas for the hot key?":** "Replicas scale *reads*, not writes — a hot *write* key still lands on one primary shard. The only way to scale writes on a single logical key is to split it into multiple physical keys, which is exactly the sub-counter fan-out."

### How do we keep the displayed count monotonic and read-your-own-writes-sane?

#### Good: max-merge of live sum and durable floor
**🗣️ Plain words:** always show the bigger of the live number and the last saved number, so it can only climb.
**Approach:** served = max(sum of sub-counters, snapshot floor). Snapshots only advance. A node loss drops the live sum, but the floor holds, so the number doesn't visibly fall.
**⚠️ What breaks:** within the staleness window, a user's own just-counted view may not appear; and corrections (fraud claw-back) must not decrease the *display*.

#### Great: monotonic display + reconciliation into the floor only
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good didn't say where corrections go; here fraud/reconciliation adjusts the *durable truth* but the *display* is clamped to never regress.
**🗣️ Plain words:** we fix the real number behind the scenes, but the public number never goes down — it just slows its rise.
**Approach:** display = max-merge clamped to the previous served value per edge. Reconciler/abuse corrections change the Cassandra total; if a correction would lower it, the display holds flat until real views catch up. Client-side optimistic +1 covers a user's own view for that user only.

**✅ Failure matrix**
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Fraud claw-back of 1M fake views | Snapshot lowered; display holds flat, doesn't drop |
| Replica lag across regions | Each region monotonic locally; converges as snapshots replicate |
| User's own view not yet counted | Client optimistic +1 for that user; global converges in seconds |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Display is always the max of the live sum and the durable floor, so it only climbs. When we claw back fraud, we lower the *true* total in storage but clamp the *display* so it never visibly drops — it just stalls until real views catch up. And a user who just watched sees their own +1 via a client-side optimistic bump, even though the global number lags a few seconds."

### How do we resist inflation / double-counting at firehose scale?

#### Bad: count every POST
**⚠️ What breaks:** trivially inflatable — a bot loop or a refresh adds unbounded views.

#### Good: dedup key + watch-signal gate
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad trusted every request; here we require a stable dedup key and minimal real-play proof.
**🗣️ Plain words:** stamp each watch once and ignore repeats, and ignore "views" with no real playback.
**Approach:** dedup on hash(viewerOrDevice, videoId, windowBucket) in Redis with 24h TTL; gate on a watch signal at ingest.
**⚠️ What breaks:** a determined attacker rotates devices/IPs; dedup store could be flooded; fail-mode of the dedup store matters.

#### Great: layered defense — gate + dedup + async behavioral scoring + reconciliation
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** dedup alone can't see *coordinated* inflation; here an async scorer catches patterns and we correct truth without false-blocking virality.
**🗣️ Plain words:** stack cheap filters first, then watch for suspicious patterns in the background, and quietly remove confirmed fraud from the real total.
**Approach:** (1) watch-signal gate; (2) per-(viewer,video) dedup TTL; (3) async scorer on the event stream flags velocity/device/geo anomalies; (4) confirmed fraud → negative deltas into the snapshot floor only. Crucially the scorer is *off the hot path* so a viral spike isn't mistaken for an attack and blocked.

**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** dedup store = 10B keys/day × 64 B = ~640 GB at 24h TTL — fits a modest Redis cluster; the dedup check adds <1ms and removes the bulk of spam before it ever reaches the log.

**✅ Failure matrix**
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Redis dedup down | Fail-open (count, small over-count) — losing real viral views is worse than a tiny inflation we later reconcile |
| Coordinated bot farm | Async scorer flags velocity/device clusters → claw back into snapshot, display clamped |
| Legit viral spike | Looks like high velocity but passes device/behavior checks → NOT blocked (off hot path) |
| Client retries (network) | Same dedupKey → idempotent no-op |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GW[Ingest gateway]
    GW -->|"gate watch signal"| Gate{has signal}
    Gate -->|"no"| Drop[Drop]
    Gate -->|"yes"| Dd[(Redis dedup)]
    Dd -->|"new"| Log[[Kafka log]]
    Log -->|"tee"| Sc[Async scorer]
    Sc -->|"fraud delta"| Snap[(Cassandra snapshot)]
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Anti-inflation is layered and cheap-first. A watch-signal gate kills no-playback spam for free, a dedup stamp collapses refreshes and retries, and then an async behavioral scorer catches coordinated farms in the background. The key design choice is keeping the scorer off the hot path, so a genuinely viral video — which *looks* like an attack by raw velocity — isn't false-blocked. Confirmed fraud is removed from the true total, never from the public display."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why fail-open on dedup?":** "Because for a public view count, briefly over-counting a few views is recoverable by reconciliation, but *blocking* real views during the exact viral moment that matters most is not. I'd rather be slightly generous and reconcile than drop the firehose."

### How do we survive overload / back-pressure on the firehose?

#### Good: bounded buffers + load shedding by priority
**🗣️ Plain words:** when we're drowning, we protect the durable log and shed the least important work first.
**Approach:** the gateway's local buffer is bounded; when Kafka is slow, the gateway applies back-pressure and **sheds in order**: first throttle non-critical read refresh (serve stale), then shed low-confidence/no-signal writes, and protect the *durable append* path last. Trigger signals: Kafka producer queue depth, consumer lag, gateway p99.
**⚠️ What breaks if unbounded:** an unbounded gateway buffer is a latent OOM outage; consumer lag with no shedding means freshness silently rots past the SLO.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Under overload I protect the durable log above all else. Buffers are bounded, and I shed in a deliberate order — first serve reads staler, then drop low-confidence writes, and only as a last resort touch the real append path. The trigger is queue depth and consumer lag, and clients hitting a limit get a 429 with a Retry-After so they back off instead of hammering."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what about exactly-once?":** "I don't promise exactly-once — at this volume it's a fiction. I promise at-least-once delivery plus idempotent dedup and a reconciled log, which is *effectively-once*. The residual drift is sub-0.1% and corrected by the batch reconciler."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean).**
- **Read path: 4 nines (99.99%)** = under ~52 minutes of downtime/year. Mechanisms: CDN edge serving (the count survives even if origin is degraded — serve last-cached), multi-AZ read tier, Redis cluster replication. **Multi-AZ** = multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically.
- **Write path: 3.5–4 nines for *acceptance*** — we must keep accepting and durably logging views; counting can lag. Mechanism: stateless gateways across AZs + gateway local-disk buffer if Kafka blips.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| CDN edge | Reads hit origin | Counts slightly slower; ~93K/s → up to 9.3M/s on origin (request-coalescing protects it) | Edge recovers; rewarm naturally |
| Read tier | Misses can't be served | Serve last-cached count (slightly staler); never an error if cache warm | Multi-AZ; autoscale |
| Redis counters | Live sum unavailable | Count shows the durable floor — looks "paused," never wrong or decreasing | Rewarm from log replay + snapshot |
| Redis dedup | Can't dedup | Fail-open: count anyway (small over-count) — no user-visible failure | Reconcile drift later |
| Kafka | Can't durably log | Gateway buffers to local disk, back-pressure; if full, shed low-confidence writes | Drain buffer when Kafka returns |
| Cassandra snapshot | No durable floor reads/writes | Reads use live sum only (slightly less safe vs decrease); flushes queue | Multi-region replicas; replay log |
| Aggregators | Counting stalls | Count "freezes" near current value (monotonic, just stale) until workers catch up | Autoscale on consumer lag |

**9C — RPO / RTO (plain definitions first).**
**RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we're OK losing if it all crashed now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw view log (Kafka) | ≈0 (acked = replicated) | minutes | Multi-AZ replication; gateway disk buffer covers blips |
| Live counters (Redis) | seconds (re-derivable) | seconds | Volatile by design; rebuilt from log + snapshot |
| Snapshot total (Cassandra) | near-0 | minutes | Multi-region replicas; tested restore |
| Displayed count | n/a (derived) | seconds | Serve floor while counters rewarm |

**9D — Cost breakdown (from capacity.py).** Total **~$0.1M/mo**, **$/1M views ~$0.38**.
1. **Cache + aggregator fleet (~$90K/mo)** — the *biggest* line item, not storage: the Redis cluster, read tier, and aggregator workers that absorb 9.3M reads/s and 463K writes/s. Compute and memory, always on.
2. **Hot event-log storage (~$16K/mo)** — 90 days of the ~270 TB/month log on hot tier.
3. **Stream ingest + cold archive (~$7K/mo)** — Kafka throughput + Glacier-tier Parquet.
**Biggest optimization:** push more reads to the **edge with a slightly longer TTL** — even nudging the hit ratio from 99% to 99.5% halves origin miss QPS and lets the read tier shrink. Counting cost is dominated by the always-on fleet, so right-sizing and aggressive batching is where the money is.

**Back-pressure:** see §8.4 — bounded buffers, shed reads-first then low-confidence writes, protect the durable append. **Observability:** golden signals — **latency** (read p99, ingest p99), **traffic** (write QPS, read QPS), **errors** (gateway 5xx, dedup fail-open rate), **saturation** (Kafka consumer lag, Redis ops/s vs ceiling, hot-key shard count). Burn-rate alert on freshness SLO (count >60s stale). **trace-id** propagated gateway → Kafka headers → aggregator → snapshot, so one view is traceable across the async hop. **Rate-limiting:** token-bucket per device/IP at the gateway; reject with **HTTP 429 + Retry-After**; the limiter state itself is a global coordinator (cross-ref §8) so it's sharded per key.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the CDN blips, counts get a little slower but never error. When the live counters die, the number just freezes at the durable floor — it looks paused, never wrong, and never drops. When Kafka hiccups, the gateway buffers to disk and keeps accepting views. The thing a user would actually notice is a count that stops climbing for a minute during an incident — and that's by design, because monotonic-but-stale beats wrong."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Eventual-consistency display vs strongly-consistent exact count**
1. **Chose & why:** approximate, near-real-time display — exact real-time counting at 10B/day is ruinously expensive and isn't what a viewer needs.
2. **Gave up:** the displayed number can lag ~60s and a user's own view isn't instantly reflected globally.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like a stadium attendance board that updates every few seconds instead of the instant each person walks in.
4. **When this reverses:** if the count drives money or a prize in real time → build a strongly-consistent per-video counter (and lose cheap horizontal scaling on hot keys).
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I made the public number a fast estimate that converges, because exact-to-the-instant counting would cost orders of magnitude more for no viewer benefit."

**🔀 Decision: Sharded sub-counters vs a single authoritative counter**
1. **Chose & why:** split hot counters so one viral video can't melt one key (the ~139K/s vs 100K/s bottleneck).
2. **Gave up:** read must sum N keys, and we need heat-tracking + reconciliation to manage drift.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** several clickers per hot turnstile instead of one, added up on demand.
4. **When this reverses:** if traffic is uniform (no hot videos) → one counter per video is simpler; drop the fan-out.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I fan the hot counter across sub-counters because you can't scale writes on a single key — the cost is summing a few keys on read, which is cheap."

**🔀 Decision: Fail-open dedup vs fail-closed**
1. **Chose & why:** if dedup is down, count anyway — losing real viral views is worse than a tiny, reconcilable over-count.
2. **Gave up:** brief over-counting during a dedup outage.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** when the bouncer's stamp machine breaks, let people in and audit later rather than turn the crowd away.
4. **When this reverses:** if counts are billing-critical → fail-closed and accept lost views.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I fail-open on dedup because under-counting a viral moment is the unforgivable error; a small over-count is recoverable by reconciliation."

**CAP / PACELC:** the core display path is **AP** — under a partition we keep serving the (possibly stale, monotonic) count rather than erroring. PACELC: **Else, we favor Latency over Consistency** — a fast near-live number beats a slow exact one. The reconciliation/snapshot path leans CP for the durable truth.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "My three least-sure calls: making the display eventually consistent, sharding hot counters, and failing open on dedup. Each trades a little exactness for scale or resilience, and each reverses if the count ever becomes money — at which point I'd build a strongly-consistent counter and accept the cost. On the core read path I'm AP and latency-first: I'd rather show a slightly stale number than no number."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ Two people watch the same video at the same instant — how do both count?**
**Mechanism:** they have different dedup keys (different viewer/device), so both events pass dedup, land in Kafka, and the aggregator batches both into a sub-counter increment of +2. No contention because we never lock a row — we atomic-increment an in-memory counter.
**🗣️ Plain words:** different viewers get different stamps, so both count; we just add them up.
**💬 One-liner:** "Concurrent views are different dedup keys, so they both increment a sub-counter — no lock, no contention."

**❓ The same user refreshes 100 times — how many views?**
**Mechanism:** the dedupKey is hash(viewer, video, windowBucket); all 100 collapse to one stamp in Redis within the 24h TTL, so it counts once.
**🗣️ Plain words:** one stamp per person per video per window — refreshing doesn't re-stamp.
**💬 One-liner:** "Same dedup key, counts once — refresh-spam is a no-op."

**❓ What happens during a synchronized premiere — your worst hot-key case?**
**Mechanism:** that one video hits ~139K/s, above the ~100K/s single-key ceiling. The heat tracker promotes it to many sub-counters and aggregators batch over 50ms windows, so each physical key sees a few hundred ops/s. Reads sum the N sub-counters.
**🗣️ Plain words:** the hot video automatically grows extra counters so no single one melts, and we add many views at once.
**💬 One-liner:** "We fan the hot counter across sub-counters and batch increments — the firehose lands on dozens of keys, not one."

**❓ What if Redis (the counters) crashes entirely?**
**Mechanism:** counters are volatile by design. The read path serves the durable Cassandra snapshot floor, so the number "pauses" at its last reconciled value — monotonic, never wrong. Counters rewarm by replaying Kafka from the last snapshot offset.
**🗣️ Plain words:** the number freezes for a bit instead of breaking, then catches up.
**💬 One-liner:** "Counters dying makes the number pause, not break — we rebuild from the durable floor and the log."

**❓ What if Kafka is down?**
**Mechanism:** the gateway buffers events to local disk and applies back-pressure; if the buffer fills, it sheds low-confidence (no-signal) writes first. When Kafka returns, the buffer drains. Acked-and-replicated events have RPO≈0.
**🗣️ Plain words:** we stash views on disk until the pipe reopens.
**💬 One-liner:** "Gateway buffers to disk and back-pressures — durable writes aren't lost, low-value ones get shed."

**❓ Can the count ever go down?**
**Mechanism:** the display is the max of live-sum and durable floor, clamped to never regress. Even fraud claw-backs lower the *true* total in storage but hold the display flat until real views catch up.
**🗣️ Plain words:** publicly it only ever climbs; we fix the real number quietly behind the scenes.
**💬 One-liner:** "Monotonic by construction — we max-merge, and corrections adjust truth, not the public number."

**❓ How do you stop a bot farm from inflating a video?**
**Mechanism:** layered — watch-signal gate, per-(viewer,video) dedup, then an async behavioral scorer that flags velocity/device/geo clusters off the hot path. Confirmed fraud becomes negative deltas into the snapshot. The scorer is async precisely so a real viral spike isn't false-blocked.
**🗣️ Plain words:** cheap filters first, pattern-detection in the background, and we quietly subtract confirmed fakes.
**💬 One-liner:** "Gate, dedup, then async scoring — fraud is removed from truth without ever blocking a real viral moment."

**❓ How do you stop a client from forging a user id or a huge count?**
**Mechanism:** identity comes from the JWT/device attestation, never a client field; the client never sends a count or a trusted timestamp — the server stamps ingest time and owns the number. Object-level authz gates the admin exact-count endpoint.
**🗣️ Plain words:** the client only says "a view happened" — we decide who and how much.
**💬 One-liner:** "Clients can't send identity, count, or time — we derive all three server-side."

**❓ Can the same view be counted twice on a retry?**
**Mechanism:** the dedupKey is the idempotency key; a retried POST hits the same stamp and returns the same 202 without re-incrementing. At-least-once delivery + idempotent dedup = effectively-once.
**🗣️ Plain words:** retries carry the same stamp, so they're free no-ops.
**💬 One-liner:** "Idempotent on the dedup key — retries can't double-count."

**❓ What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?**
**Mechanism:** the always-on cache + aggregator fleet (~$90K/mo) dwarfs storage. Biggest lever: push more reads to the edge with a slightly longer TTL — 99%→99.5% hit ratio halves origin miss QPS and shrinks the read tier.
**🗣️ Plain words:** the live fleet costs more than the data; serve more from the edge.
**💬 One-liner:** "It's the fleet, not the data — nudge the cache hit ratio and the fleet shrinks."

**❓ How do you keep the raw log from growing forever?**
**Mechanism:** ~270 TB/month; 90-day hot retention via time-partitioned segments dropped O(1), cold tiered to S3/Glacier Parquet. PII erasure via crypto-shredding on the immutable log.
**🗣️ Plain words:** old data ages out instantly by dropping whole partitions, and goes to cheap cold storage.
**💬 One-liner:** "Partition-drop at 90 days, tier the rest to object storage — never row-by-row deletes."

**❓ How would you add likes / shares / watch-time counters later?**
**Mechanism:** they're structurally identical counters — same ingest, dedup, sharded-counter, snapshot pattern with a different event type. Watch-time is a *sum* not a count, but the aggregation tier already does batched folds, so it generalizes to sum/avg.
**🗣️ Plain words:** likes and shares are just more counters on the same machine.
**💬 One-liner:** "Same pipeline, new event type — the counter platform is generic."

**❓ How would you support a real-time exact count for a contest?**
**Mechanism:** that's a different consistency tier — a strongly-consistent per-video counter (serialized increment log or consensus group) for the contest videos only, accepting the lower throughput and higher cost on those specific keys. The general firehose stays approximate.
**🗣️ Plain words:** we'd carve out the few contest videos onto a strict, slower counter.
**💬 One-liner:** "Carve the contest videos onto a strongly-consistent counter — pay for exactness only where it's needed."

**❓ How does freshness stay within 60s under load?**
**Mechanism:** the freshness SLO is monitored as consumer lag; a burn-rate alert fires before 60s, and aggregators autoscale on lag. Under extreme overload we shed reads (serve staler) before letting counting fall further behind.
**🗣️ Plain words:** we watch how far counting is behind and add workers before it slips.
**💬 One-liner:** "Freshness is consumer lag — we alert and autoscale on it, and shed reads before writes."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A view count service is a thing that counts how many times each video was watched, near-real-time, at billions a day, without being easy to fake. The design splits into two halves. The read half serves about nine million count-reads a second mostly from a CDN edge with a short TTL, falling back to a stateless service that sums a video's sub-counters in Redis and max-merges them against a durable Cassandra floor — so the number is fast and only ever climbs. The write half swallows about half a million increments a second into a durable Kafka log and counts them asynchronously in batches; the hardest problem is one premiere video doing a hundred-and-forty-thousand a second against a single counter that tops out near a hundred thousand, solved by fanning that one logical counter across adaptive sub-counters and batching increments. The two halves connect through Kafka — the same log that feeds the counters also feeds an abuse scorer and a batch reconciler that keep the durable truth honest. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: capture durably and instantly, count approximately and asynchronously, and never let the public number be wrong or go down."
